label: fiction
title: The Sunday Painter
charge: Fraud — selling a forged old-master panel as genuine
elements: The accused knew it was when she sold it.

beats.text: A television connoisseur testifies with relish: his pigment analysis found titanium white in the panel — a pigment unavailable before the twentieth century. 'An instant fake.'
beats.reveal_note: The pigment is real and modern — and sits only in a repair layer. He sampled a patch without asking what the conservation file would have told him.

beats.text: The defence produces a public gallery's conservation file: a documented 1974 restoration of flake damage, with the repair site diagrammed — precisely where the prosecution took its sample.
beats.reveal_note: The boring paper trail decides it. Modern pigment in a documented modern repair is exactly what an honest old panel looks like.

beats.text: The prosecution traces the panel's ownership and finds a hole: no record of it anywhere between 1939 and 1952.
beats.reveal_note: A mid-century gap is the rule, not the exception, in honest collections — wars scatter paperwork. A gap is an absence of evidence, not evidence of forgery.

beats.text: A conservator shows the court a cross-section from the panel: original ground and paint layers under centuries of craquelure, with the modern pigment confined to the repair.
beats.reveal_note: Quiet corroboration: the panel's body is old; only its bandage is new.

twist: The 'instant fake' was an old panel with a documented 1974 repair — the modern pigment sat exactly where the gallery's own conservation file said it would. The loud expert sampled the bandage and called the patient a fraud.
